turns.content: I'm reviewing this Rust function and the compiler is complaining about missing cases even though I feel my logic covers everything. My colleague wrote a pattern that matches any value but it seems to fail at compile time.

The issue is likely that your pattern isn't truly exhaustive because Rust requires every possible variant in an enum to be covered by a match arm. If you use the underscore wildcard, it must come after all specific patterns unless you explicitly allow non-exhaustive crates.
I tried putting _ at the end but still got an error saying I missed a case. It feels like Rust is being overly strict compared to Java where missing cases might just be warnings.

Rust enforces this strictly because it's core safety; if you can't exhaustively match, the program could panic unexpectedly. You need to ensure every variant is either matched explicitly or grouped with | in a single arm pattern.
So I should restructure my match so all cases are in one group using | and then use _ for anything else?

Exactly, you can group patterns with | to reduce repetition. But if your enum has many variants, consider defining a trait or using non-exhaustive crates like derive_more if appropriate. For simple enums, just list each variant once.
